Notice Of Petition To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings Pending Arbitration
Off Calendar Mar-20-2018 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Jennifer A. Huber, Esq. with Cecily Harris, Esq. of Keker, Van Nest & Peters LLP, T:415.773.6668, for defendant/moving party;
Nanci E. Nishimura, Esq. of Cotchett, Pitre & McCarthy LLP, T:650.697.6000, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT LYFT, INC.'s Petition To Compel Arbitration And Stay Proceedings Pending Arbitration, as follows:

Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s petition to compel arbitration and stay proceedings pending arbitration is granted. Plaintiff Sarah Johnson acknowledges that she accepted Lyft's Terms of Service. The Terms of Service include a clear and unmistakable delegation clause that requires the arbitrator to decide the validity and applicability of the arbitration agreement except in very limited circumstances that are not present in this case. Ms. Johnson fails to demonstrate that the delegation clause, separate from the arbitration agreement, is unconscionable. While procedural unconscionability exists because the delegation clause was presented in a take-it-or-leave-it manner, there is no substantive unconscionability. The delegation clause is not overly harsh, and does not sanction one-sided results. (Aanderud v. Superior Court (2017) 13 Cal.App.5th 880, 892; Tiri v. Lucky Chances, Inc. (2014) 226 Cal.App.4th 231, 247.). Therefore, the arbitrator must decide whether Ms. Johnson's claims are the proper subject for arbitration.

ORDER SIGNED.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600, email: torreanom@aol.com; mtorreano@sftc.org.
